edges.description: The ingested cyanide load must be detoxified, a process dependent on dietary sulfur amino acids.
edges.source: Cyanogenic Glucoside (Linamarin) and Cyanide Exposure from Bitter Cassava
edges.target: Impaired Cyanide Detoxification with Low Sulfur Amino Acid Intake

edges.description: Inadequately detoxified cyanide and its metabolites injure upper motor neurons via excitotoxic and oxidative mechanisms.
edges.source: Impaired Cyanide Detoxification with Low Sulfur Amino Acid Intake
edges.target: Upper Motor Neuron and Corticospinal Tract Injury

edges.description: Loss of upper motor neuron / corticospinal input produces a permanent spastic paraparesis of the legs.
edges.source: Upper Motor Neuron and Corticospinal Tract Injury

nodes.description: The initiating exposure of konzo is high dietary intake of the cyanogenic glucoside linamarin from insufficiently processed bitter cassava roots. During digestion linamarin is hydrolysed by the enzyme linamarase to cyanohydrins, which break down to toxic cyanide, so a monotonous diet of poorly processed bitter cassava delivers a high chronic cyanide load. Epidemiological studies associate urinary linamarin and its cyanide metabolite thiocyanate with the occurrence of konzo, and outbreaks cluster geographically and seasonally with high cyanide exposure.
nodes.id: Cyanogenic Glucoside (Linamarin) and Cyanide Exposure from Bitter Cassava

evidence.reference_title: Uptake of wetting method in Africa to reduce cyanide poisoning and konzo from cassava.
evidence.snippet: Cassava contains cyanogenic glucosides which are hydrolysed by an enzyme linamarase to produce cyanohydrins which breakdown to toxic cyanide.

evidence.reference_title: Geographical and seasonal association between linamarin and cyanide exposure from cassava and the upper motor neurone disease konzo in former Zaire.
evidence.snippet: urinary linamarin, the cyanogenic glucoside and source of cyanide in cassava, was more closely associated with the occurrence of konzo

nodes.description: Cyanide is normally detoxified to thiocyanate by sulfurtransferase enzymes that require a sulfur donor supplied by dietary sulfur-containing amino acids (methionine, cysteine). In konzo-affected populations the bitter-cassava diet is protein-poor and deficient in sulfur amino acids, so a high cyanide intake is paired with low capacity to detoxify it. Children with konzo have measurably lower cyanide detoxification rates than controls, and worse nutritional status (stunting/wasting) distinguishes konzo patients from equally cyanide-exposed healthy individuals, implicating impaired detoxification and malnutrition as the decisive co-factors.
nodes.id: Impaired Cyanide Detoxification with Low Sulfur Amino Acid Intake

evidence.reference_title: Etiology of Konzo, epidemic spastic paraparesis associated with cyanogenic glycosides in cassava: role of thiamine deficiency?
evidence.snippet: High cyanide consumption with low dietary sulfur intake due to almost exclusive consumption of insufficiently processed bitter cassava roots was proposed as the cause of Konzo

evidence.reference_title: Lower sulfurtransferase detoxification rates of cyanide in konzo-A tropical spastic paralysis linked to cassava cyanogenic poisoning.
evidence.snippet: the odds of developing konzo was reduced by 63%

evidence.reference_title: Revisiting Konzo Risk Factors in Three Areas Differently Affected by Spastic Paraparesis in Eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo Discloses a Prominent Role of the Nutritional Status-A Comparative Cross-Sectional Study.
evidence.snippet: The only factor differentiating konzo patients from healthy individuals exposed to cyanide poisoning appeared to be their worse nutritional status.

nodes.description: The clinical endpoint of konzo is an abrupt-onset, symmetric, permanent and non-progressive spastic paraparesis of the lower limbs, with upper motor neuron signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, extensor plantar responses, clonus) and a stiff, scissoring gait, and no sensory or genitourinary impairment. Once established the deficit is irreversible.

evidence.reference_title: Konzo: a distinct neurological disease associated with food (cassava) cyanogenic poisoning.
evidence.snippet: The main clinical picture consists of a symmetrical, permanent and irreversible spastic paraparesis (motor neuron disease) with no signs of sensory or genitourinary impairments.
Konzo is characterized by the abrupt onset of isolated and symmetric spastic paraparesis of the lower extremities which is permanent but nonprogressive.

nodes.description: Inadequately detoxified cyanide and its metabolites selectively injure upper motor neurons and their corticospinal (pyramidal) tract axons, with sparing of lower motor neurons, sensory pathways, and sphincter function. Proposed effector mechanisms include excitotoxicity and oxidative stress: cyanide is a mitochondrial toxin, and cassava-cyanide metabolites (thiocyanate, cyanate) act on neurons, with cyanate inducing reactive oxygen species, caspase activation, and depolarizing currents through ionotropic glutamate receptors in spinal cord neurons.
nodes.id: Upper Motor Neuron and Corticospinal Tract Injury

evidence.reference_title: Konzo risk factors, determinants and etiopathogenesis: What is new? A systematic review.
evidence.snippet: Konzo is a toxico-nutritional upper motor neuron disease causing a spastic paraparesis in schoolchildren and childbearing women in some African countries.

evidence.reference_title: New insight into the molecular etiopathogenesis of konzo: Cyanate could be a plausible neurotoxin contributing to konzo, contrary to thiocyanate.
evidence.snippet: OCN (and SCN to a lesser extent) induced depolarizing currents in primary spinal cord neurons, through an activation of ionotropic glutamate receptors.

evidence.reference_title: New insight into the molecular etiopathogenesis of konzo: Cyanate could be a plausible neurotoxin contributing to konzo, contrary to thiocyanate.
evidence.snippet: konzo, a tropical spastic paraparesis due to selective upper motor neuron damage
